# Readiness Probes: Why Dependency State Doesn't Belong in `/readyz`

The semantic split between mission-capability misconfiguration (the probe
signal) and runtime dependency state (the metric signal), and the
Kubernetes/Prometheus interaction that makes the difference matter.

> Prescriptive checklist (what gates `/readyz`, where to put dependency
> gauges) and antipatterns live in
> [standards/observability/readiness-probes.md](https://github.com/jewzaam/standards/blob/main/observability/readiness-probes.md).

## Pattern

| Signal type                              | Where it goes                          | Operator alert path                |
|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Mission-capability misconfiguration**  | `/readyz` returns 503 → NotReady       | `kube_pod_status_ready` in Prom    |
| **Process up?**                          | `/healthz` returns 200/503             | Pod restart on 503 (liveness)      |
| **Runtime dependency state** (upstream API sad, token currently 401, cache stale, write-back disabled) | Gauge / counter on `/metrics` | PromQL alert on the metric          |

`/readyz` returns 503 ONLY when misconfiguration prevents the service from
doing its job (credentials missing, required config not loadable, required
output directory unwritable at startup). Once those startup prerequisites
are satisfied, the probe stays at 200 for the rest of the process lifetime —
even if every downstream call is failing.

Runtime dependency sadness lives in metrics, never in the probe.

## Why the split

Kubernetes drops a `NotReady` Pod from Service endpoints. Under
`ServiceMonitor`-style scraping (which targets Pods via the Service), a
flapping `/readyz` makes the Pod intermittently unscrapeable. Prometheus
loses exactly the signal the operator needs to debug the dependency problem
that caused the readiness flap in the first place.

Liveness (`/healthz`) is a separate axis: it returns 200 while the process
is alive. A 503 here triggers a Pod restart, which won't fix a
dependency-side problem either — the new Pod will hit the same sad upstream
and fail the same way.

The clean split:

- **Misconfig → NotReady.** Kubernetes-native signal. Alert via
  `kube_pod_status_ready` or equivalent.
- **Process broken → liveness fails.** Pod restart. Last resort.
- **Dependency sadness → metric.** Prometheus-native signal. Alert via
  PromQL on the exporter's own gauges and counters
  (`*_fetch_success_total`, `*_credentials_writable`,
  `*_last_fetch_timestamp_seconds`, etc.).

Operators using pod-level scrape (`role: pod`) still get metrics during
cold-start because pod-level scrape ignores Service endpoint membership.
This is a reason to prefer pod-level scrape for exporters.

## Provenance

Surfaced while designing
<https://github.com/jewzaam/claude-quota-exporter> — the initial `/readyz`
design gated on first successful upstream fetch. That would have hidden
cold-start credential failures behind a flapping probe and suppressed
exactly the metrics the operator needed to diagnose the problem.

## Reference

- Kubernetes probe configuration:
  <https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness-readiness-startup-probes/>
- Prometheus Operator `ServiceMonitor` (endpoint-based scrape):
  <https://prometheus-operator.dev/docs/operator/api/#monitoring.coreos.com/v1.ServiceMonitor>
